hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth- to early-twentieth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a pair, they retain their shared roofline, mirrored facades, brick cladding with decorative brickwork, flat headed and segmentally arched openings, and vertical orientation including projecting bay windows set under gables. 55 Oxford also retains its decorative woodwork at the gable including brackets, window surround, shingles, and bargeboard. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: One-way | Residential | Fine-grain Victorian rowhouses | Quiet

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows, bay window.

facade_description: 53 Oxford St presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as mixed, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows, bay window. HCD note: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth- to early-twentieth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a pair, they retain their shared roofline, mirrored facades, brick cladding with decorative brickwork, flat headed and segmentally arched openings, and vertical orientation including projecting bay windows set under gables. 55 Oxford also retains its decorative woodwork at the gable including brackets, window surround, shingles, and bargeboard. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
